title: TypeScript Utility Types
summary: Partial, Required, Readonly, Pick, Omit, Record, Exclude, Extract, ReturnType and NonNullable — the standard library of type transformations.

questions.prompt: What does `Partial<User>` produce if `User = { name: string; age: number }`?
choices: { name?: string; age?: number }  — all properties optional
{ name: string | undefined; age: number | undefined }
Partial is not a valid TypeScript type
questions.explanation: `Partial<T>` adds `?` to every property — making them optional. This is not the same as `| undefined`, though `?` does include undefined in practice. The key difference: `?` means the key can be absent entirely.

questions.prompt: Why is `Partial<T>` commonly used for update/patch function parameters?
choices: Because you can't pass a full object to a function
Because a patch only contains the fields that changed — you shouldn't need to provide every field
TypeScript requires Partial for any function that mutates
questions.explanation: An update operation like PATCH in REST only sends changed fields. `Partial<User>` lets you pass `{ age: 31 }` without providing `name` and `email`. Without Partial, you'd need to provide all fields even when only one changed.

questions.prompt: Does this compile?
choices: Compiles — Readonly is shallow; it prevents reassigning scores but not mutating the array
Error — you cannot call push on Readonly arrays
questions.explanation: `Readonly<T>` is shallow. `u.scores = []` is an error (reassignment blocked). But `u.scores.push(4)` mutates the existing array through its reference — Readonly doesn't protect nested objects. Use `ReadonlyArray<number>` for the array itself.

questions.prompt: Pick<User, 'name' | 'email'> with User = { id, name, email, password } produces:
choices: { id: string; name: string; email: string; password: string }
Error — cannot Pick more than one key
questions.explanation: Pick keeps only the listed keys. `'name' | 'email'` means only those two survive. All other properties (`id`, `password`) are dropped from the resulting type.

questions.prompt: Omit<User, 'password'> differs from Pick in that:
choices: Omit specifies what to REMOVE (blacklist), Pick specifies what to KEEP (whitelist)
Omit only works with single keys
They are identical — just different names
questions.explanation: Pick = whitelist: list what you want. Omit = blacklist: list what you don't want. With many fields, Omit can be cleaner when you just want to hide one field (like password). Pick is cleaner when you want only a few of many fields.

questions.prompt: What type is `statusMap` forced to contain?
choices: Error — 'error' key is missing; Record requires all keys in the union
Compiles — Config is just Record<string, string>
Error — Status cannot be used as a Record key
questions.explanation: `Record<Status, string>` creates `{ idle: string; loading: string; error: string }`. All three keys are required. Missing `error` is a compile error. This is Record's superpower — it enforces at compile time that every case is handled.

questions.prompt: Exclude<'a' | 'b' | 'c', 'a'> produces:
questions.explanation: `Exclude<T, U>` removes from T the members assignable to U. Removing `'a'` from `'a' | 'b' | 'c'` leaves `'b' | 'c'`.

questions.prompt: Extract<MouseEvent | KeyboardEvent | FocusEvent, MouseEvent> produces:
questions.explanation: `Extract<T, U>` keeps ONLY the members of T assignable to U. `MouseEvent` is assignable to `MouseEvent`, so only it survives. The others are excluded.

questions.prompt: What is the type of `clean`?
questions.code:
type MaybeString = string | null | undefined;
type clean = NonNullable<MaybeString>;
questions.explanation: `NonNullable<T>` removes `null` and `undefined` from T. `MaybeString = string | null | undefined` → after NonNullable: `string`.

questions.prompt: ReturnType<typeof getUser> extracts:
choices: The return type of the getUser function
The type of the getUser variable
ReturnType requires a generic argument, not typeof
questions.explanation: `ReturnType<F>` takes a function type F and extracts what it returns. `typeof getUser` converts the value `getUser` to its type before passing to `ReturnType`. This avoids re-declaring the return type manually.

questions.prompt: What is `AddParams`?
questions.code:
function add(a: number, b: number): number { return a + b; }
type AddParams = Parameters<typeof add>;
choices: Parameters<add> would be correct; typeof is wrong
questions.explanation: `Parameters<F>` extracts parameter types as a labeled tuple. `add(a: number, b: number)` → `[a: number, b: number]`. This is a tuple, not an array — order and count are fixed.

questions.prompt: Which utility types operate on union types (not object shapes)?
questions.explanation: `Exclude`, `Extract`, and `NonNullable` all filter union type members. `Partial` transforms object shape properties — it doesn't make sense on a union.

questions.prompt: A colleague writes `Record<string, User>` for a user cache. What hidden assumption does this make?
choices: All users have the same id
Every possible string key maps to a full User — accessing any key appears valid to TypeScript, even if it doesn't exist
questions.explanation: `Record<string, User>` tells TypeScript every string key exists and has type `User`. No `undefined` in the value type. Accessing `cache['nonexistent']` returns `User` to TypeScript — but `undefined` at runtime. Fix: `Record<string, User | undefined>` or enable `noUncheckedIndexedAccess`.

questions.prompt: What does `Awaited<Promise<Promise<string>>>` produce?
questions.explanation: `Awaited<T>` recursively unwraps nested Promises. `Awaited<Promise<Promise<string>>>` → `Awaited<Promise<string>>` → `string`. This is how TypeScript infers the resolved value of chained or nested async operations.

questions.prompt: What type does context have here?
questions.explanation: `DB['connect']` is an indexed access type — it gets the type of the `connect` property, which is `(host: string, port: number) => void`. `Parameters` of that function type is `[host: string, port: number]`.

questions.prompt: DeepPartial is NOT a built-in because:
choices: The standard Partial<T> is intentionally shallow — deep versions require custom recursive type aliases
questions.explanation: `Partial<T>` only makes the top-level properties optional. Nested objects remain fully required. A recursive `DeepPartial<T>` must be written manually using conditional/mapped types. It's not built-in because deep mutability semantics are complex.

questions.prompt: Which utility type would you use to model all the possible state keys for a status reducer (where the same enum drives both the type and the config)?
choices: Record<Status, StateConfig> — enforces every status has a config entry
questions.explanation: `Record<Status, StateConfig>` creates an object type where each member of the Status union is a required key. TypeScript flags missing cases at compile time — if you add a new status and forget its config, it's an immediate error.

questions.prompt: Which utility types require `typeof fn` (not just `fn`) when applied to a function?
questions.explanation: `ReturnType<F>`, `Parameters<F>`, and `InstanceType<C>` take type arguments. `fn` is a value — you need `typeof fn` to get its type. `Partial` takes an object type, not a function type.

questions.prompt: What is the type of `safeUser`?
choices: { id: string; name: string; password: string; createdAt: Date }
questions.explanation: `Omit<User, 'password' | 'createdAt'>` removes both `password` and `createdAt`. What remains is `{ id: string; name: string }`. This is the canonical 'safe for serialization' pattern — strip sensitive fields before sending to the client.

questions.prompt: You receive a function `fn: (a: number, b: string) => boolean`. How do you type a wrapper that has identical signature without manually redeclaring it?
choices: Infer the types manually and redeclare
Use Parameters<typeof fn> for args and ReturnType<typeof fn> for return type
questions.explanation: `(...args: Parameters<typeof fn>) => ReturnType<typeof fn>` creates a perfectly matching wrapper signature. If `fn` is updated, the wrapper's type updates automatically — no manual sync needed. This is the standard pattern for decorators, logging wrappers, and memoization.

flashcards.back: Makes ALL properties optional. Shallow only — nested objects stay required. Use for update/patch function params where caller provides only changed fields.
Pick<T, K> = whitelist: keep only K. Omit<T, K> = blacklist: keep everything except K. Use Pick for 'I want a few fields', Omit for 'I want to hide one field'.
Creates `{ [key in K]: V }`. Enforces ALL keys in K are present. Use for exhaustive mappings like `Record<Status, Config>` — adding a new Status causes a compile error.
Both filter union members. Exclude<T, U> REMOVES members assignable to U. Extract<T, U> KEEPS members assignable to U. Mnemonic: extract = pull out what you want.
Extracts the return type of a function. Requires `typeof fn` (value → type). Use in wrappers to avoid manually syncing return type declarations.
Shallow immutability only. `Readonly<{ arr: number[] }>` prevents re-assigning `arr`, but NOT `arr.push()`. Use `ReadonlyArray<number>` for array contents.
Removes `null` and `undefined` from T. `NonNullable<string | null | undefined>` = `string`. Use after null-checks when you know the value exists.
Recursively unwraps nested Promises. `Awaited<Promise<Promise<string>>>` = `string`. Essential for typing async utility functions and middleware chains.

project.brief: You are building a user API with a `User` interface: `{ id: string; name: string; email: string; password: string; createdAt: Date; role: 'admin' | 'user' | 'guest' }`. Design the TypeScript types for four API operations using only utility types (no inline object types): 1) GET /users/:id — returns a user safe for the client (no password). 2) POST /users — accepts all fields except id and createdAt (those are server-generated). 3) PATCH /users/:id — accepts any subset of fields except id and createdAt. 4) A role-config map where each role must have a `{ label: string; permissions: string[] }` entry — adding a new role must be a compile error if this map isn't updated. For each, write the type alias and the utility type expression that creates it.

checklist.text: GET response: Omit<User, 'password'> or Pick with the safe fields
PATCH body: Partial<Omit<User, 'id' | 'createdAt'>> — nested utility types
Role config: Record<User['role'], { label: string; permissions: string[] }>
Explains that Record<User['role'], ...> auto-updates when role union changes

hints: GET: Omit<User, 'password'> removes the password. The id and timestamps are safe to expose.
POST: server generates id and createdAt — Omit<User, 'id' | 'createdAt'>.
PATCH: combine Omit and Partial — Partial<Omit<User, 'id' | 'createdAt'>>.
Record key: `User['role']` is an indexed access type that extracts `'admin' | 'user' | 'guest'` — so adding 'moderator' to the role union immediately flags the Record as incomplete.
